extractions.problem: Stem cell potency assessment lacks standardized methods; functional assays are cumbersome and molecular markers show discrepancies across cell types.
extractions.method: Review of functional assays (teratoma, blastocyst chimerism, tetraploid complementation) and in silico diagnostics including genomic profiling (DNA microarrays), bioinformatics algorithms (Stem Cell Matrix, PluriTest, ScoreCard), and epigenetic/metabolic marker evaluation.
extractions.finding: Pluripotency is a dynamic state regulated by many molecular factors; bioinformatics approaches (PluriTest, ScoreCard) show promise but functional assays remain the gold standard; no single molecular signature reliably predicts pluripotency across all cell types.
extractions.dataset: Human ESCs, iPSCs, multipotent and unipotent stem cells
extractions.baseline: Functional assays (teratoma formation, blastocyst chimerism, tetraploid complementation)

extractions.problem: Accurate modeling of blood flow requires cell-level simulation of red blood cells, but existing constitutive models for RBC membranes perform poorly at high shear rates.
extractions.method: HemoCell parallel computing framework using lattice Boltzmann method for plasma flow coupled with immersed boundary method for RBC membranes; a new constitutive material model based on strain energy formulation replacing WLC-based spectrin-link model.
extractions.finding: The new material model accurately reproduces apparent viscosity (Fåhræus–Lindqvist effect) across vessel diameters 20–128 μm and captures solid-like behavior of dense RBC suspensions at low shear rates; it outperforms the spectrin-link model at shear rates over 1,500 s⁻¹.
extractions.dataset: Simulated blood flow in straight vessels D=20–128 μm, hematocrit 20–45%, ~2×10⁴ cells
extractions.metric: Relative apparent viscosity vs vessel diameter
extractions.baseline: Coarse-grained spectrin-link model (Fedosov et al. 2010); experimental data from Pries et al. 1992
extractions.result: Good agreement with Pries et al. experimental viscosity curves; improved accuracy at shear rates >1,500 s⁻¹; scalable to 10⁶ cells on 8,192 cores

extractions.problem: The metabolic changes underlying clonal variation in monoclonal antibody-producing CHO cells are poorly understood, and the link between metabolic burden and recombinant protein productivity remains unclear.
extractions.method: Kinetic metabolic model for CHO cells with regulatory terms (hexokinase inhibition by G6P, PGI inhibition by PEP) calibrated against intracellular/extracellular metabolite profiles from shake flask batch cultures; simulation of intracellular fluxes and biomarker ratios.
extractions.finding: Clone-to-clone metabolic variation is more significant than the effect of recombinant protein expression; the high-producer clone shows efficient glucose utilization, increased pyruvate dehydrogenase flux, and higher TCA cycle flux with increased anaplerosis via pyruvate carboxylase.
extractions.dataset: CHO-Cum2 parental, high-producer, and low-producer clones (cumate-inducible anti-CD20 mAb), shake flask batch cultures over 6 days
extractions.metric: Intracellular flux distributions, ATP/ADP ratio, NADH/NAD ratio, extracellular metabolite concentrations
extractions.baseline: Parental (non-producing) CHO cell line; model without regulatory terms
extractions.result: mAb production accounts for at most 5% of total carbon uptake; regulatory terms (HK inhibition by G6P + PGI inhibition by PEP) significantly reduced simulation error for glucose and nucleotide ratios

extractions.problem: Linear B-cell epitope prediction methods show poor and inconsistent performance, and many existing tools are unavailable online, hindering vaccine design efforts particularly during COVID-19.
extractions.method: Benchmarking of 7 CLI-available B-cell epitope predictors (BcePred, BepiPred, ABCpred, COBEpro, SVMTriP, LBtope, LBEEP) on standardized datasets; development of consensus classifiers combining per-residue and per-peptide predictions.
extractions.finding: Individual predictors show modest performance (MCC 0.01–0.08); the consensus classifier achieved the best per-residue MCC and accuracy; BepiPred had the best per-peptide MCC; ~45% of published predictor web servers are no longer available.
extractions.dataset: Consensus_NR dataset (tens of thousands of epitope/non-epitope peptide sequences, 20-residue window)
extractions.baseline: Individual predictors (BcePred, BepiPred, ABCpred, COBEpro, SVMTriP, LBtope, LBEEP)
extractions.result: Per-peptide best MCC=0.0778 (BepiPred); per-residue consensus MCC=0.0489; consensus accuracy=55.59% (per-peptide), 53.04% (per-residue)

extractions.problem: Bulk-tissue DNA methylomes average over many cell types, obscuring cell-type-specific contributions to disease; existing computational deconvolution methods are limited to blood.
extractions.method: EpiSCORE algorithm imputing cell-type-specific DNA methylation reference matrices from tissue-specific scRNA-seq atlases using promoter DNAm–mRNA anticorrelation; weighted robust partial correlation (wRPC) for cell-type fraction estimation in bulk methylomes.
extractions.finding: The DNAm-atlas covers 13 solid tissue types and 40 cell types with >90% validation accuracy in 8/13 tissues; it correctly predicts cancer cell of origin, discovers prognostic associations in olfactory neuroblastoma and stage 2 melanoma, and identifies neuronal origin for schizophrenia-associated differential methylation enriched for GWAS risk loci.
extractions.dataset: 13 human solid tissue types, 40 cell types; TCGA bulk DNAm data; independent single-nucleus DNAm datasets
extractions.metric: Validation accuracy, tumor purity correlation (vs ESTIMATE, ABSOLUTE, LUMP), prognostic association (survival)
extractions.baseline: ESTIMATE, ABSOLUTE, LUMP for tumor purity; existing blood-only deconvolution methods
extractions.result: Validation accuracy >90% for 8/13 tissues; tolerates up to 30–40% imputation error rate; novel prognostic subtypes identified in ONB and melanoma

extractions.problem: Designing efficient non-fullerene acceptors (NFAs) for organic solar cells with improved near-infrared absorption and photovoltaic properties beyond the Y21 reference molecule.
extractions.method: DFT and TDDFT at B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level to calculate band gap, exciton binding energy, transition density matrix, reorganization energy, absorption maxima, and open-circuit voltage of four novel Y-series NFAs (YA1–YA4) with modified end-capped acceptors.
extractions.finding: YA1 shows the best photovoltaic properties among designed molecules with the lowest energy gap, smallest reorganization energies, highest absorption maxima, and fine open-circuit voltage; all designed molecules outperform the Y21 reference.
extractions.dataset: Y21 reference molecule and 4 designed NFAs (YA1–YA4); PM6 donor polymer
extractions.metric: HOMO-LUMO gap, λmax, reorganization energy (λe, λh), open-circuit voltage (Voc), exciton binding energy
extractions.baseline: Y21 (experimentally synthesized, 15% efficient NFA, λmax=741 nm)
extractions.result: YA1: energy gap=1.924 eV, λe=0.0073 eV, λh=0.0083 eV, λmax=783.36 nm (gas)/715.20 nm (CHCl₃), Voc=1.17 V

extractions.problem: Whether anthocyanins can inhibit immune checkpoint proteins PD-1 and PD-L1 in colorectal cancer, thereby activating anti-tumor immune response in the tumor microenvironment.
extractions.method: In vitro cell viability assays (MTS) on HCT-116 and HT-29 colorectal cancer cells with pure anthocyanins; molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) for PD-1/PD-L1 binding; Western blot and immunofluorescence for PD-L1 expression; flow cytometry for PD-1 on PBMCs in mono- and co-culture.
extractions.finding: Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (C3G) showed highest molecular docking affinity for PD-1 and PD-L1; C3G decreased PD-L1 fluorescence by 39% and PD-1 expression in PBMCs by 26–55% depending on culture conditions; D3G was the only effective anthocyanin for direct cancer cell inhibition.
extractions.dataset: HCT-116 (microsatellite instable), HT-29 (chromosomal instability) colorectal cancer cells; human PBMCs
extractions.metric: IC50, binding energy (kcal/mol), PD-L1 fluorescence intensity, PD-1 expression (%)
extractions.baseline: Atezolizumab and pembrolizumab (clinical immune checkpoint inhibitors); untreated controls
extractions.result: C3G PD-1 docking: −6.8 kcal/mol, PD-L1: −9.6 kcal/mol; D3G IC50: 396±23 (HCT-116), 329±17 μg/mL (HT-29); C3G decreased PD-L1 by 39%, PD-1 by 26–55%

extractions.problem: Identifying novel inhibitors of COP9 signalosome (CSN)-associated kinases CK2 and PKD that can stabilize tumor suppressor p53 and induce apoptosis in tumor cells.
extractions.method: In silico 2D/3D conformer screening of ~10⁶ compound database using curcumin and emodin as lead structures (3D RMSD superposition + 2D Tanimoto similarity); Lipinski's rule-of-five filtering; QSTR toxicity prediction; in vitro kinase assays and apoptosis assays (annexin V-FITC, DNA fragmentation, caspase activity).
extractions.finding: 35 compounds identified by in silico screening; 7 compounds (3 curcumin-group including piceatannol, 4 emodin-group including anthraquinone) potently inhibited CSN-associated kinases, increased p53 levels, and induced apoptosis in tumor cells.
extractions.dataset: In-house database of ~10⁶ compounds; HeLa tumor cells
extractions.metric: Kinase inhibition (% activity), Tanimoto coefficient, RMSD, p53 levels, annexin V binding, DNA fragmentation, caspase activity
extractions.baseline: Curcumin and emodin (known CSN kinase inhibitors)
extractions.result: 7/35 compounds confirmed as potent kinase inhibitors; 2D similarity threshold <85% (scaffold hoppers successfully identified); all compounds passed Lipinski and toxicity filters

extractions.problem: Predicting drug sensitivity of cancer cells to targeted therapies using genomic profiling, to enable personalized treatment stratification.
extractions.method: In silico tumor model (Cellworks v7.3) comprising >4,700 biological entities, ~6,500 reactions, ~25,000 kinetic parameters covering major signaling pathways; genomic profiles (copy number variation) used to create simulation avatars; predictions compared against empirical drug sensitivity data.
extractions.finding: The in silico model accurately predicted ~85% of gene mutation–drug sensitivity associations from the Garnett et al. study and achieved ~75% agreement with in vitro experimental findings on patient-derived GBM cell lines.
extractions.dataset: 45/639 cancer cell lines from Garnett et al. study; patient-derived GBM cell lines (Mayo Clinic); 70/130 drugs, 51/84 genes
extractions.metric: Agreement rate (% concordance between in silico predictions and experimental results)
extractions.baseline: Garnett et al. empirical drug sensitivity screening (hypothesis-independent); in vitro experimental data
extractions.result: ~85% retrospective agreement (22/25 testable associations); ~75% prospective agreement with in vitro GBM data

extractions.problem: Whether in silico hiPSC-derived cardiomyocyte models can reliably predict drug-induced ionic current block effects compared to adult ventricular cardiomyocyte models, for cardiac safety pharmacology.
extractions.method: In silico action potential models (Paci2013 for hiPSC-CMs, O'Hara-Rudy for hAdultV-CMs) simulating four degrees of selective block (0.1×IC50, IC50, 2×IC50, full) of eight major cardiac transmembrane currents (INa, ICaL, If, IK1, IKr, IKs, Ito, INaCa).
extractions.finding: hiPSC-CMs and hAdultV-CMs show qualitatively similar responses to ionic current block, but hiPSC-CMs are quantitatively more sensitive to ICaL block (up to 4× greater APD shortening) and IK1 block (repolarization failure) due to NCX overexpression and reduced repolarization reserve; IKr block effects are nearly identical at therapeutic concentrations.
extractions.dataset: In silico ventricular-like and atrial-like hiPSC-CM models; hAdultV-CM (O'Hara-Rudy endocardial) model
extractions.metric: APD30, APD50, APD70, APD90, MDP, AP amplitude, dV/dtmax
extractions.baseline: hAdultV-CM O'Hara-Rudy model; experimental data from Ma et al. 2011
extractions.result: IKr block: nearly identical APD prolongation at 0.1×IC50 and IC50; ICaL block: APD shortening up to 4× larger in hiPSC-CM; IK1 block: repolarization failure in hiPSC-CM but not hAdultV-CM
